nodes.title: First Aid Introduction & Disclaimer

runs.text: never a substitute for professional medical treatment

items.text: in seconds during a high-stress disaster.
. Never assume a casualty is "fully recovered" just because they wake up or stop bleeding.
No matter how successfully you apply these first aid steps,
every casualty must be evaluated by a real doctor
. Always call emergency services or transport the casualty to a hospital.
The life-saving protocols are adapted directly from certified medical manuals: the
Singapore Red Cross Standard First Aid Manual
Malaysia Pantai Hospitals IHH Healthcare Essential Guide to First Aid
St. John Ambulance Canada First Aid Reference Guide

children.title: First Aid
children.subtitle: Immediate, temporary assistance provided to a casualty before professional medical help arrives.

children.title: Conscious Choking
children.subtitle: Emergency clearing of a completely blocked airway in adults, children, and infants.

nodes.subtitle: Life-saving techniques used during cardiac arrests or respiratory emergencies

nodes.title: Medical Information
nodes.subtitle: Basic medical information for first-aiders to assist you in case that you fainted.

children.title: Fill In Basic Information Section
children.subtitle: Basic information for identification and ease for blood transfusion when needed.

children.title: Fill In Allergies Section
children.subtitle: Additional information aiding emergency responders to avoid triggering your allergies due to some treatments.

nodes.title: Evacuation Bag (Go-bag)

children.title: Backpack
children.subtitle: A sturdy, hands-free backpack

children.title: Water & Food Supplies
Bottled Water (Min 2 Liters per pax)
children.subtitle: Immediate, safe hydration for the first 24 hours of evacuation.

children.title: High-Calorie Protein Bars (6+ per pax)
children.subtitle: Dense, lightweight calories that can be eaten while walking.

children.title: Personal Water Filter (1 per pax)
children.subtitle: Allows you to safely drink from rivers or puddles by filtering out most of the bacteria.

children.title: Water Purification Tablets
children.subtitle: Chemical drops/tablets to kill viruses in highly contaminated water.

children.title: Emergency Ration Bars
children.subtitle: Vacuum-sealed 3600-calorie blocks designed to withstand extreme heat/cold for 5 years.

children.title: Electrolyte Powder Packets
children.subtitle: Prevents dehydration and muscle cramps if you are sweating heavily.
The core fuel to keep you hydrated and energized for 3 days on the move.

children.title: Important Documents, Cash & Hygiene
Cash (Small Denominations)
children.subtitle: To buy gas, food, or a hotel room when electronic payment systems are down.

children.title: Physical Copies of ID/Passports (per pax)
children.subtitle: Stored in a waterproof pouch to prove identity if wallets are lost.

children.title: Wet Wipes & Hand Sanitizer
children.subtitle: Cleaning hands before eating or treating wounds when running water isn't available.

children.title: Feminine Hygiene Products
children.subtitle: Crucial for comfort and sanitation for females.

children.title: Travel Toothbrush & Toothpaste (per pax)
children.subtitle: Maintaining basic dental hygiene helps prevent infections and boosts morale.

children.title: USB Flash Drive
children.subtitle: A digital backup containing insurance policies, house deeds, and family photos.
The administrative and sanitary items needed to recover after the disaster.

children.title: First-aid Kit
Wound Care & Dressings
Adhesive Plasters (Assorted Sizes)
children.subtitle: For minor cuts, blisters, and fingertip injuries. Include waterproof options.

children.title: Sterile Gauze Pads (Large & Small)
children.subtitle: Highly absorbent pads placed directly over heavy bleeding or scrapes.

children.title: Surgical Tape
children.subtitle: To tape down gauze pads or secure rolled bandages in place.

children.title: Roller Bandages
children.subtitle: Lightweight, highly stretchy rolls used simply to hold a sterile gauze pad securely in place.

children.title: Elastic Bandages
children.subtitle: Thicker, firm-stretch rolls used to apply compression to a sprained wrist or ankle to reduce swelling.

children.title: Triangular Bandage
children.subtitle: A large piece of fabric used to create an arm sling, secure a splint, or act as a makeshift tourniquet.
Core supplies to stop bleeding, seal open wounds, and support physical injuries.

children.title: Tools & Protective Equipment
Disposable Nitrile Gloves
children.subtitle: Protects from cross-infection. (Nitrile is better than latex due to common allergies). Pack 3+ pairs.

children.title: CPR Face Shield
children.subtitle: Provides a safe, one-way barrier if you need to give rescue breaths during CPR.

children.title: EMT Trauma Shears
children.subtitle: Heavy-duty scissors that can safely cut through thick denim or leather to reach a hidden wound.

children.title: Foil Space Blanket
children.subtitle: Reflects and traps body heat to prevent hypothermia and treat clinical shock after trauma.

children.title: Fine-Tipped Tweezers
children.subtitle: For removing splinters, glass shards, ticks, or scraping away insect stingers.

children.title: Safety Pins
children.subtitle: Excellent for securing triangular bandages or fastening torn clothing out of the way.
Hardware you need to treat a casualty safely and effectively.

children.title: Cleaning & Medications
Antiseptic Wipes
children.subtitle: To easily disinfect minor scrapes and clean your hands before treating a wound.

children.title: Pain Relief (Paracetamol/Ibuprofen)
children.subtitle: Reduces fever, manages headaches, and reduces tissue swelling after a sprain.

children.title: Antibiotic/Antiseptic Cream
children.subtitle: Applied to minor cuts before bandaging to actively prevent bacterial infection.

children.title: Antihistamines
children.subtitle: Crucial for treating mild to moderate allergic reactions from insect stings, foods, or plants.

children.title: Saline Wash Pods
children.subtitle: Squeezable pods of sterile salt water to flush dirt out of deep wounds or wash chemicals out of eyes.

children.title: Aspirin (300mg)
children.subtitle: Can be chewed by a casualty experiencing a suspected heart attack to thin the blood (if not allergic).

children.title: Hydrogel/Burn Dressings
children.subtitle: Actively cools minor burns without sticking (clean, cool running water is the essential alternative).

children.title: Hydrocortisone Cream
children.subtitle: A topical steroid cream to reduce severe itching from insect bites or poison ivy.
Sterilization fluids, topical creams, and basic over-the-counter drugs.
The medical supplies needed to handle trauma and illness when there are no doctors around.

children.title: Light, Power & Communication
Headlamp or Flashlight (1 per pax)
children.subtitle: Navigating safely at night. (Headlamps are superior as they keep hands free).

children.title: Emergency Whistle (1 per pax)
children.subtitle: To loudly signal for rescue if trapped. It takes much less energy to blow a whistle than to scream.

children.title: Spare Batteries
children.subtitle: Powering your lights and radios when charging is impossible.

children.title: Power Bank & Charging Cable
children.subtitle: Giving your smartphone 1 or 2 extra charges to coordinate with others.

children.title: Physical Local Map & Compass
children.subtitle: Navigating evacuation routes when cell service and GPS are completely offline.

children.title: Battery Radio/Hand-Crank Radio
children.subtitle: To receive official government broadcasts and weather updates when cell networks are down.

children.title: Chemical Glow Sticks
children.subtitle: Provides safe, long-lasting light that won't ignite gas leaks (great for kids).
Navigating the dark and staying connected when the power grid completely fails.

children.title: Shelter, Warmth, Clothing & Tools
Windproof Matches or Lighters
children.subtitle: Starting a fire for warmth, signaling, or boiling unpurified water. Store in a waterproof container.

children.title: Heavy-Duty Rain Poncho (1 per pax)
children.subtitle: Keeps you and your backpack dry in a downpour.

children.title: Spare Wool Socks & Underwear (2 sets per pax)
children.subtitle: Trench foot and infections happen quickly in wet conditions. Wool retains heat even when wet.

children.title: N95/Dust Masks (2 per pax)
children.subtitle: Protects your lungs from toxic dust, smoke, and debris.

children.title: Emergency Mylar Sleeping Bag/Bivvy
children.subtitle: Reflects 90% of your body heat back to you. Much better than a flat space blanket because it seals out the wind.

children.title: Multi-Tool (eg. Swiss Army Knife)
children.subtitle: Includes pliers, knife, and screwdrivers for minor repairs and opening cans.

children.title: Duct Tape (Small Roll)
children.subtitle: Repairing ripped clothing, patching a torn tent, or taping broken windows.

children.title: Work Gloves (Leather/Thick)
children.subtitle: Moving sharp rubble or hot debris without slicing your hands open.
Protecting your body from the elements, building a fire, and handling physical obstacles.

nodes.subtitle: The grab-and-go backpack you need if you have to evacuate immediately.

nodes.title: Family Action Plan

children.title: Pick a Fire Meeting Spot
children.subtitle: Decide on one spot outside (like the neighbor's driveway) where everyone meets if the smoke alarm goes off.

children.title: Locate the Breaker Box
children.subtitle: Show everyone where the main electrical panel is and how to flip the main switch if water starts leaking into the house.

children.title: Locate the Water Valve
children.subtitle: Show everyone how to turn off the main water supply to stop a burst pipe from flooding the home.

children.title: Create a Group Chat
children.subtitle: Have a dedicated family emergency WhatsApp/group chat so everyone can check in instantly during a bad storm.

nodes.subtitle: Making sure everyone knows what to do in a normal emergency.

nodes.title: The Stay-at-Home Kit (Power/Water Outage)

children.title: Food, Water & Off-Grid Cooking
Bottled Water (Min 12 Liters per pax)
children.subtitle: A 3-day supply (4L per day per person) for drinking, brushing teeth, and light washing.

children.title: Ready-to-Eat Pantry Food
children.subtitle: Canned soup, crackers, beans, and peanut butter. Things you can eat unheated.

children.title: Manual Can Opener
children.subtitle: So you can actually open those cans when the power is out.

children.title: Large Trash Bags
children.subtitle: If garbage trucks are delayed due to flooded roads, this keeps the house from smelling.

children.title: Portable Butane Stove
children.subtitle: To heat food/water if needed in case of electric stove not working.

children.title: Butane Gas Canisters
children.subtitle: The stove is useless without fuel. Keep 3 to 4 spare butane cans to make coffee or boil noodles.
The essential calories and hydration to keep you fed when the fridge and taps fail.

children.title: Power, Light & Backup Energy
High-Capacity Power Banks
children.subtitle: Keep them fully charged so you can keep your phones alive to check weather updates.

children.title: Battery-Powered Lanterns/Lights
children.subtitle: Much safer than candles (which are a severe fire hazard) for lighting up the living room.

children.title: Spare Batteries
children.subtitle: For your flashlights and lanterns.

children.title: Battery-Powered Fan
children.subtitle: A lifesaver if the AC goes out during a hot, humid storm.

children.title: Portable Generator
children.subtitle: An inverter generator to run a fridge or fan. Safety note: Always run generators outdoors.

children.title: Fuel
children.subtitle: Store fuel in proper jerry cans away from the house for the generator.
Keeping the lights on, phones charged, and air circulating during a grid failure.

nodes.subtitle: Getting through a 3-day blackout or water disruption comfortably when a storm knocks out the grids.

nodes.title: Vehicle Emergency Kit (Breakdowns & Floods)

children.title: Safety & Escape
Seatbelt Cutter & Window Breaker
children.subtitle: To escape if your car gets caught in a sudden flash flood. Store in the center console, not the trunk.

children.title: Reflective Warning Triangle
children.subtitle: Put this behind your car so nobody rear-ends you in heavy rain.

children.title: Portable Jump Starter/Jumper Cables
children.subtitle: To jumpstart your car (or help someone else) if the battery dies.

children.title: Tire Inflator
children.subtitle: Plugs into the 12V cigarette lighter to temporarily fix a flat so you can drive to a shop.
Tools to safely exit a trapped vehicle or secure your perimeter on a busy roadside.

children.title: Comfort & Weather
Umbrella/Rain Poncho
children.subtitle: So you don't get completely soaked if you have to change a tire in a storm.

children.title: Bottled Water (Min 2 Liters) & Snacks
children.subtitle: To keep you sane and hydrated if you are stuck in gridlock traffic for 5 hours due to blocked roads.

children.title: Mini First Aid Kit
children.subtitle: A small pouch with plasters and painkillers for basic headaches or scrapes on the road.
Basics to stay dry and hydrated while waiting out severe traffic or a tow truck.

nodes.subtitle: Handling a dead battery, a flat tire, or getting caught in a flooded street.

nodes.title: Survival Tips Introduction & Disclaimer

runs.text: never a substitute for live, localised emergency instructions.

items.text: Removed all the lengthy background reading so you can access
, but you must actively search online or tune into official local channels for
, active weather alerts, and localised threat levels.
No matter what a general survival checklist says,
. If told to evacuate, go immediately.
The life-saving protocols in this section are strictly

from leading emergency management and meteorological agencies, including the Federal Emergency Management Agency (FEMA), the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) and international Civil Defence protocols.

children.title: Survival Guide
children.subtitle: Basic survival tips to aid your survivability during natural disasters and incidents.

children.subtitle: Timeless rules for staying alive when completely cut off from civilization.

nodes.subtitle: The overarching rules and baseline survival tactics for when things go wrong.

nodes.title: Water Disasters & Floods

runs.text: Do not wait for official evacuation orders if water is visibly rising.

items.text: Avoid retreating to a residential attic
against the ceiling as water rises.

runs.text: Never walk, swim, or drive through moving water.
Never enter a flooded basement or room if the power is still on.
